coredata.dc:title: Novel ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode biosensors for Cu2+ and biothiols detection based on norepinephrine modified carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaoping
Zhuang, Yafeng
Chen, Jing
Lin, Jianwei
Chen, Jinghua
Han, Zhizhong
coredata.dc:description: Novel norepinephrine modified carbon dots (NECDs) were prepared by one-step method. Compared with unmodified carbon dots (CDs), the fluorescence emission peak of NECDs was red shifted and the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield were improved. A ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform has been developed for detecting Cu2+ and biothiols based on NECDs. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) works as chromogenic agent in the sensing system, and OPD is oxidized to 2, 3-diaminobenzoazine (oxOPD) in the presence of Cu2+. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between NECDs and oxOPD, the fluorescence of CDs at 450 nm is effectively quenched, but a new emission peak of oxOPD at 563 nm is formed. By adding l-cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH), their sulfhydryl groups bind to Cu2+, thus inhibiting the oxidation of OPD and retaining the fluorescence of CDs. In addition, Cu2+, GSH and Cys can be quantitatively detected by the absorption change of solution. Moreover, the color change is visible. The dual-mode detection method has high sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of Cu2+ and biothiols. The biosensors are successfully applied in actual samples, providing a new way for the detection of ions and biothiols in the environment and biology in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetric
Cu2+
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Cu2+ and GSH in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM and (B) HRTEM diagram of NECDs, the inset of Fig. 1A shows the particle size distribution of NECDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of NECDs. Insert: images of NECDs under UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence spectra under different concentrations of Cu2+ and the relationship of lgI r with Cu2+ concentration. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and corresponding linear graphs at different concentrations of Cu2+.

Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding relationship under different concentrations of (A) GSH and (C) Cys. UV–vis absorption spectra and corresponding linear graphs at different concentrations of (B) GSH and (D) Cys.
The effects of various ions on (A) fluorescence and (B) absorption. The effects of amino acids and other compounds on (C) fluorescence and (D) absorption.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of different samples. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of oxOPD and the fluorescence spectra of NECDs. (C) The fluorescence lifetime of NECDs + oxOPD with and without Cu2+.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence and colorimetric biosensors for ratiometric detection of Cu2+ and biothiols.
Cu2+ measurement in tap water with ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry.
GSH measurement in serum by ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry.

head.ce:title: Novel ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode biosensors for Cu2+ and biothiols detection based on norepinephrine modified carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research, Fuzhou 350122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel norepinephrine modified carbon dots (NECDs) were prepared by one-step method. Compared with unmodified carbon dots (CDs), the fluorescence emission peak of NECDs was red shifted and the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield were improved. A ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform has been developed for detecting Cu2+ and biothiols based on NECDs. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) works as chromogenic agent in the sensing system, and OPD is oxidized to 2, 3-diaminobenzoazine (oxOPD) in the presence of Cu2+. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between NECDs and oxOPD, the fluorescence of CDs at 450 nm is effectively quenched, but a new emission peak of oxOPD at 563 nm is formed. By adding l-cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH), their sulfhydryl groups bind to Cu2+, thus inhibiting the oxidation of OPD and retaining the fluorescence of CDs. In addition, Cu2+, GSH and Cys can be quantitatively detected by the absorption change of solution. Moreover, the color change is visible. The dual-mode detection method has high sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of Cu2+ and biothiols. The biosensors are successfully applied in actual samples, providing a new way for the detection of ions and biothiols in the environment and biology in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in various fields such as biosensors, bioimaging and optoelectronics due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, easy functionalization, stability and photobleaching resistance [1–3]. Generally, the surface of synthesized CDs has many functional groups (–COOH, –OH), resulting in poor selectivity in detection, especially in ion detection. In order to improve its performance and expand its application, many researchers have modified CDs. For example, Wang et al. enhanced the emission properties of CDs by modifying CDs with GSH and then improved their applicability in biological imaging and fluorescence sensing [4]. Yan's group modified CDs with 2-(4-aminophenyl) benzothiazole (ABT), which has a higher quantum yield and becomes more stable. The CDs-ABT can be used to detect berberine hydrochloride through the mechanism of internal filtration effect (IFE) [5]. Therefore, we modified CDs to improve its performance and expand its application in the biological field.

At present, Cu2+ is a metal ion widely present in industrial, environmental and biological systems. With the development of industrialization, the pollution caused by Cu2+ is becoming more and more serious, so the environmental monitoring of Cu2+ has received great attention [6]. In addition, Cu2+ also plays an important role in the human body [7]. Excessive Cu2+ can cause serious diseases, such as kidney disease and neurodegenerative diseases [8]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) allows Cu (II) in drinking water at a level of 20 μM [9]. In addition, thiol compounds such as GSH and Cys, play an important role in human physiological processes [10]. Imbalance of biothiols levels is closely related to the occurrence of diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, liver disease and even cancer [11,12]. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a method for the detection of Cu2+ and biothiols.

So far, many methods have been used to detect Cu2+ and biothiols, including electrochemistry [13,14], fluorescence [15], colorimetry [16] and so on. Among these methods, fluorescence and colorimetry have the advantages of simple operation and low cost. However, most of the detection methods for Cu2+, GSH and Cys mentioned above all use single detection mode, which is often affected by background interference factors such as light source and concentration. The fluorescence intensity ratio of two different peaks can reduce the interference caused by environmental effects and improve the sensitivity [17]. The combination of colorimetry and fluorescence can further restrain the influence to improve the accuracy of the signal [6]. In addition, colorimetric method may achieve visual monitoring. Based on the advantages of the above two methods, it is very meaningful to design biosensors with dual-mode colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence.

Therefore, we use simple one-step hydrothermal method to modify CDs with NE, and the fluorescence properties of the NECDs are improved, the fluorescence emission peak is redshifted, and the quantum yield is increased. In addition, we constructed dual-mode biosensors for ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cu2+ and biothiols. As shown in Scheme 1 , OPD is catalyzed to generate oxOPD in the presence of Cu2+, and oxOPD quenches NECDs fluorescence by fluorescence energy resonance effect (FRET), while the fluorescence intensity of oxOPD itself is enhanced. In the presence of GSH or Cys, OPD oxidation is inhibited and NECDs fluorescence is retained. In addition, oxOPD has absorption at 418 nm, so with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the biosensors change from colorless to yellow under sunlight. With the addition of biothiols, Cys and GSH, the absorption value of the solution at 418 nm decreases, and the solution changes from yellow to colorless. Therefore, fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cu2+ and biothiols are established. The dual-mode biosensors have been successfully used to detect Cu2+ and GSH in actual samples, which have broad practical application prospects.

0.84 g citric acid, 0.2 g polyoxyethylene bis(amine), 536 μL polyvinyl polyamine and 0.76 g norepinephrine was dissolved in 20 mL of ultra-pure water. The mixture was placed in an autoclave for reaction at 200 °C for 5 h, filtered and purified by dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da) for 24 h, and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm/min. The supernatant was taken and stored at 4 °C for later use at a concentration of 11.5 mg·mL−1. The synthesis procedure of unmodified CDs was described in supplementary materials.

The obtained NECDs were diluted 30 times before follow-up tests. To detect Cu2+, firstly, 10 μL 250 mM OPD solution, 950 μL PB buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 8.5) and 10 μL Cu2+ solution of different concentrations were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. Then 30 μL NECDs solution was added and mixed uniformity, and the emission spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and UV–vis absorption spectra were scanned in the range of 320–600 nm. (The fluorescence intensity of NECDs was I 1, and that of oxOPD was I 2).

For the detection of Cys or GSH, Cu2+, OPD and PB buffers were added, then added 50 μL Cys or GSH solution of different concentrations and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. The following steps were the same as the detection steps of Cu2+, and the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded.

To verify the selectivity of the biosensors for Cu2+, we tested some common ions with the designed biosensors. The detection steps are as follows, 10 μL 250 mM OPD solution, 950 μL PB buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 8.5) and 10 μL various ionic solutions (10 μM) were mixed, and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. Then 30 μL NECDs solution was added and mixed evenly, and fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded.

Similarly, we verified the selectivity of the designed biosensors for Cys or GSH by detecting some reducing substances, amino acids and HCY using the biosensors. The experimental procedure was the same as 2.3, and Cys or GSH in the procedure was replaced with substances above 50 μM.

In order to verify the accuracy of the biosensors, the contents of Cu2+ and GSH in the actual sample were detected, and the labeling recovery experiment was carried out. Tap water was collected from city water supply pipes and serum was purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., ltd. Different concentrations of Cu2+ and GSH were added to the tap water sample solution and serum solution respectively, and the samples were tested by the above method.

The TEM image (Fig. 1 A) exhibits that NECDs are spherical shape and monodisperse. The size distributes between 2.5 and 4.4 nm. The HRTEM diagram of NECDs (Fig. 1B) shows the well-resolved lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane lattice of graphite, verify the crystalline structure.

As shown in Fig. 1C, NECDs has absorption peaks at 240 nm, 308 nm and 354 nm, and its maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength are located at 361 nm and 451 nm, respectively. The insert in Fig. 1C shows an image of NECDs under a 360 nm UV lamp, which shows an intense dark blue fluorescence. Compared with the unmodified CDs (Fig. S1A and 1B) at the same concentration, NECDs have a new absorption peak at 308 nm, which can be attributed to the conjugated system formed by the connection between CDs and NE. And its fluorescence emission peak is redshifted 20 nm and its fluorescence intensity is enhanced compared with CDs. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield of the unmodified CDs is 31.8 %, which increases to 40 % after modification (Fig. S1C).

The molecular structure of NECDs was characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. S1D, the characteristic peaks at 3434 cm−1 and 3270 cm−1 are attributed to OH and NH stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1600 cm−1 corresponds to CO. The peak at 1450 cm−1 is NH bending vibration, and the peaks of CO, CC and CN are 1015 cm−1, 1107 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1, respectively. Compared with that of CDs, the characteristic peak of CO for NECDs is offset, and there is a new characteristic peak of N-CO at 1560 cm−1, and the peak at 1107 cm−1 becomes smaller. These spectral results further indicate that CDs was modified with NE successfully.

In order to further characterize the surface functional groups of NECDs and verify whether NE has modified CDs, NECDs is characterized by XPS. As can be seen from Fig. S2A, NECDs have three peaks at 286.01 eV, 399.55 eV and 532.11 eV belonging to C (62.28 %), N (11.83 %) and O (19.09 %), respectively. The high resolution C 1 s spectrum has three main peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 287.47 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO and HO-CO/N-CO, respectively (Fig. S2B). The XPS spectrum of N 1 s (Fi. S2C) shows four peaks at 398.71 eV, 399.29 eV, 399.98 eV and 400.9 eV, which may be attributed to pyridine N, C-NH2, pyrrolic N and graphite N. In addition, the XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. S2D) can be decomposed into two peaks corresponding to HO-CO/N-CO (530.79 eV) and COC/C-OH (530.79 eV), respectively. Compared with that of CDs, the peak positions are all changed, with an increased proportion of O and an enhanced proportion of CO in the C1s and O1s spectra. The combined FTIR and XPS results indicate that NECDs were successfully synthesized.

To obtain the optimized sensing performance, the reaction time, pH and OPD concentrations were optimized. Firstly, we investigated the fluorescence intensity ratio of NECDs and oxOPD after Cu2+ was added. As can be seen from Fig. S3A, lgI r (I r = I 1/I 2) changes little with the increase of reaction time. So it can be detected at any time after adding Cu2+. Secondly, the concentration of OPD was selected and the difference of lgI r was measured at 100 mM, 150 mM, 200 mM, 250 mM, 300 mM for the presence or absence of OPD. As shown in Fig. S3B, the difference of lgI r is the largest when OPD concentration is 250 mM. This may be because the generated oxOPD reaches the strongest quenching effect on the prepared NECDs solution at this concentration. Finally, the influence of pH on the constructed biosensors was investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. S3C. When pH = 8.5, the difference of lgI r is the largest. Since Cu2+ is more easily hydrolyzed to form hydroxide under higher alkaline conditions, which inhibits the oxidation of OPD.

As shown in Fig. 2 A and Fig. 2B, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, colorless OPD is oxidized into yellow oxOPD, which causes the quenching of the fluorescence of NECDs and the formation of a new emission peak at 563 nm. At the same time, the absorption value at 418 nm increases in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. In the fluorescence method, the linear relationship between lgI r and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.1–10 μM is lgI r = -0.103C Cu 2++1.36, and the correlation coefficient is 0.998, the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.0408 μM. In the colorimetric method (Fig. 2B), in the range of 0.5–8 μM, the absorbance value has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+, A = 0.112C Cu 2++0.074 (R 2 = 0.996), and the LOD is 0.18 μM. As shown in Table S1, compared with other methods, the fluorescence and colorimetric analytical methods established in this study have lower LOD and higher sensitivity, which can be used for quantitative detection of Cu2+.

In Fig. 3 A and Fig. 3C, in the presence of fixed amounts of Cu2+, oxOPD production decreased with the addition of Cys or GSH, leading to a subsequent recovery of fluorescence from NECDs and a decrease in fluorescence from oxOPD themselves. I r shows a good linear relationship with GSH concentration in the range of 0.5–40 μM. The linear relationship is I r = 0.461C GSH + 2.051. In the range of 1–40 μM, the linear relationship between I r and Cys concentration is I r = 0.327C Cys + 2.196 (R 2 = 0.987). The LODs of GSH and Cys are 0.24 μM and 0.31 μM, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3B and Fig. 3D, in colorimetric method, when a certain amount of GSH or Cys was added, the absorption value of the solution at 418 nm decreased, and the color of the solution changed from yellow to colorless. In the range of 1–10 μM, the absorption of oxOPD displays a linear relationship with GSH concentration. The linear equation is A = −0.066C GSH + 1.413, and LOD is 0.65 μM. Compared with other sensors for GSH detection by colorimetric and fluorescence methods, the constructed biosensors have a lower LOD as shown in Table S2. The linear relationship between the concentration of Cys and the absorption value of oxOPD in the range of 1–10 μM is A = -0.056C Cys + 1.29, and LOD is 0.52 μM.

As can be seen from Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity and absorption value do not change significantly after the addition of the above interfering ions, demonstrating that the constructed biosensors have high selectivity for Cu2+. In the GSH and Cys selectivity experiment, we selected some amino acids such as Arg, Lys, Glu, Gly, Tyr, the reductive substances glucose, AA and HCY which belongs to sulfhydryl compounds as interference substances. Under the same conditions of GSH or Cys detection, as shown in Fig. 4C, the influence of the above interfering substances on the fluorescence biosensors is small and can be ignored. In colorimetric method (Fig. 4D), the above interfering substances have a certain influence on the biosensors, but the change is smaller compared with GSH and Cys. It is hard to distinguish the GSH and Cys by the designed biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ and GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy of the biosensors, the ratiometric fluorescence method and colorimetric method were used to determine Cu2+ and GSH in the actual samples. Table 1 exhibits that Cu2+ was not detected in tap water by the ratiometric fluorescence method, and the recoveries are 100.8 %–106.8 %. The colorimetric method has the recoveries between 94.4 % and 105.4 %. Since the concentration of GSH in human body is higher than that of Cys, only GSH in serum was determined. As shown in Table 2 , the GSH content in serum measured by both methods is 1.63 μM. After adding different concentrations of GSH solution, the recoveries are 100.0 %–106.2 % for the fluorescence method and 91.1 %–103.2 % for the colorimetric method, and RSDs are less than 5.0 %. Based on the above results, it declares that the constructed biosensors have good accuracy and can be used for the detection of Cu2+ and GSH in real samples.

In order to study the mechanism of the biosensors, firstly we studied the UV–vis absorption spectra of different samples (Fig. 5 A and Fig. S4). NECDs and NECDs with OPD only have an absorption peak at 361 nm attributed to NECDs. The systems including Cu2+ and OPD all show an absorption peak at 418 nm. However, the mixture of NECDs and Cu2+ does not have the absorption peak at 418 nm. This proved that the absorption is due to the oxidization of OPD with Cu2+. After adding Cys and GSH, the absorption is droppedsignificantly, meaning that there is interaction between biothiols and Cu2+. In addition, Fig. 5B displays that the absorption peak of the generated oxOPD overlaps significantly with the fluorescence emission peak of NECDs, indicating the existence of internalization effect (IFE) or fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET) between oxOPD and NECDs. To further confirm the mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of NECDs + OPD is measured to be 10.27 ns, while it is reduced to 9.32 ns after Cu2+ is added (Fig. 5C), confirming that the fluorescence quenching of NECDs is due to FRET between NECDs and oxOPD. In the presence of biothiol compounds such as GSH or Cys, the fluorescence recovery of NECDs is mainly due to the inhibition of OPD oxidation by GSH or Cys through strong chelation of sulfhydryl group and Cu2+ [18], which reduces the production of oxOPD and FRET effect between NECDs and oxOPD, thus maintain NECDs fluorescence. In a word, Cu2+ oxidizes OPD to generate oxOPD, which quenches the fluorescence of NECDs, while the fluorescence intensity of oxOPD itself is enhanced. In the presence of GSH or Cys, the oxidation of OPD is inhibited, so that the fluorescence of NECDs is restored.

In this work, NE modified CDs were successfully synthesized via one-step method. The fluorescence performance of NECDs is improved and the emission peak is red-shift. The “on–off-on” colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode biosensors based on NECDs were developed to detect Cu2+ and biothiols. The mechanism study declares that Cu2+ can oxidize colorless OPD to yellow oxOPD, which can quench the blue fluorescence of NECDs and provide a new yellow fluorescence. Cu2+ forms a chelating structure with biothiols, resulting in a weaker oxidisability. Consequently, the fluorescence of NECDs restores and the color of the solution disappears. Thus, the signal is varied with Cu2+ and biothiols. The LODs of Cu2+, GSH and Cys are 0.18 μM, 0.24 μM and 0.31 μM, respectively. Meaningfully, the designed dual-mode biosensors have been successfully applied to the detection of target in tap water and serum with good sensitivity and selectivity. In conclusion, the designed biosensors have an extensive application prospect in biological and environmental detection of Cu2+, GSH or Cys.

Xiaoping Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yafeng Zhuang: Investigation. Jing Chen: Validation. Jianwei Lin: Data curation. Jinghua Chen: Conceptualization. Zhizhong Han: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602053), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019J01300), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2017Y9122).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107828.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Lightwave Technol.

Anal. Chim. Acta
